title: SEGMENT 3 — The first clinical wins: cultured skin and cultured cartilage

lines.text: SEGMENT 3 — The first clinical wins: cultured skin and cultured cartilage
The earliest products of tissue engineering didn't come from the Langer-Vacanti paradigm directly.
They came from a parallel tradition — cell culture for clinical use — that predates the term "tissue engineering" by about a decade.
The two early clinical successes are cultured autologous skin for burn patients, and cultured autologous chondrocytes for cartilage repair.
Both work.
Both have been used in patients for decades.
Both are humble compared to the dream of a printed liver.
And both are real wins that deserve to be understood before we get to the more glamorous stuff.
The skin story starts with Howard Green, a researcher at MIT and later Harvard, who in the mid-1970s figured out how to grow human keratinocytes — the main cell type of the epidermis — in dishes.

The trick involved co-culturing the keratinocytes with a layer of irradiated mouse fibroblasts called 3T3 cells, which provided growth factors and a kind of metabolic support that the keratinocytes needed.
Without the 3T3 layer, the keratinocytes died.

With it, they thrived, and crucially, they could be expanded enormously — Green's group showed that a small biopsy of skin from a patient could be expanded, over a few weeks, into enough cultured epithelium to cover the patient's entire body.
That's an enormous amplification — something like ten thousand-fold — and it's exactly what you'd want if you were trying to treat a severe burn.
The clinical application came in 1981.
Two brothers, ages 5 and 6, were caught in a fire that burned over 95% of their bodies.
With burns that extensive, conventional treatment — taking small skin grafts from the patient's own unburned areas, the so-called "donor sites" — was impossible.
There simply wasn't enough unburned skin left to cover the wounds.
The two boys were referred to a Boston hospital that had a relationship with Green's lab.
The doctors took small biopsies from the boys' unburned areas.
Green's lab expanded those biopsies into sheets of cultured epithelium, big enough to cover the boys' bodies.
The sheets were grafted onto the burn wounds.
The boys lived.
That outcome was reported in the New England Journal of Medicine in 1984 and is widely considered the first clinical use of a tissue-engineered product in humans.
The cultured skin product went on to be commercialized as Epicel, which is still on the market today, used in patients with massive burns.
It's worth understanding its limitations, because they tell you something important about the field.
Epicel is essentially just epidermis — the outer layer of skin.
It doesn't include dermis, the deeper layer with blood vessels and nerves and hair follicles and sweat glands.
So Epicel-grafted skin lacks the full functional repertoire of normal skin.
It's also fragile — the sheets are only a few cell layers thick, hard to handle, easy to tear.
And it requires several weeks of culture to grow enough — weeks during which a severely burned patient is in critical condition.
Despite these limitations, Epicel saves lives.
It is the product of the field that has been in continuous clinical use the longest.
It is, in many ways, the proof that the whole approach works.
The cartilage story follows a similar arc.
Cartilage is, biomechanically, a magnificent tissue — it has to bear enormous loads, has to glide against other cartilage surfaces with almost zero friction, and has to last a lifetime.
It also has a notorious failure mode: when cartilage is damaged, it doesn't heal.
There are no blood vessels in cartilage to bring in repair cells.
There are very few chondrocytes — the cartilage cells themselves — and they don't divide much.
A torn meniscus, a worn-out knee cartilage, a focal defect after an injury — these things don't fix themselves.
They tend to progress to osteoarthritis, which is why so many adults eventually need joint replacements.
In the late 1980s, a group in Sweden led by Lars Peterson and Mats Brittberg developed a procedure called autologous chondrocyte implantation, or ACI.
The procedure goes like this.
You take a small biopsy of healthy cartilage from a non-load-bearing area of the patient's knee — the so-called "trochlear notch" — during an initial arthroscopy.
You ship the biopsy to a central lab.
The lab digests the cartilage, isolates the chondrocytes, and expands them in culture for several weeks until you have many millions of cells.

Then, in a second surgery, you open the patient's knee, debride the cartilage defect, sew a small flap of periosteum — the membrane covering bone — over the defect, and inject the cultured chondrocytes underneath the flap.

The cells settle into the defect, lay down new cartilage matrix, and over the course of months to years, the defect is filled with regenerated cartilage that resembles, more or less, the original tissue.
The Peterson group published their first clinical results in 1994.
The product was commercialized as Carticel by a company called Genzyme, and in 1997, Carticel became the first cell therapy approved by the U.
S.
FDA.
The first.
Not the first tissue-engineered product — Epicel preceded it as a commercial offering — but the first cell therapy formally approved under the FDA's biologics pathway.
That regulatory milestone is part of why the cartilage work is historically important.
It set the precedent for how cell-based products would be regulated in the United States, a framework that's still in use for everything from CAR-T cells to bioprinted tissues.

Carticel and its successors — including newer matrix-induced versions where the chondrocytes are seeded onto a collagen scaffold before implantation, which makes the surgery easier and the outcomes a bit better — have been used in tens of thousands of patients.
The outcomes are good, not miraculous.
Most patients have less pain and better function.
The regenerated tissue is sometimes more fibrous than true hyaline cartilage, which can affect long-term durability.
The procedure is expensive.
The indications are limited.
But it works, in a population that previously had no good options between conservative management and joint replacement.
What I want you to take away from these two early stories is that the recipe works.

Scaffold (which in the case of cultured epithelium is just a substrate sheet, and in matrix-induced ACI is a collagen mesh), plus cells (autologous, expanded), plus bioactives (the right culture conditions, sometimes growth factors during maturation), equals a functional replacement tissue that the patient's body accepts and uses.
The recipe is real.
The early wins prove it.
The harder question — which we're about to get into — is how far you can push it.
